Editorial Design · Print Layout · Content Design

Content Design & Layout
for Zoo Magazine

Managing and designing print content, editorial layouts, magazine covers, and a landmark archive section for Zoo Magazine — a high-end fashion and culture publication founded in Berlin. Working across Issues 80 and 81, every page was crafted to uphold the magazine's signature luxury aesthetic while ensuring clear, readable editorial flow.

Client

Zoo Magazine — Amsterdam

Role

Graphic & Editorial Designer

Issues

Issue 80 & Issue 81

Tools

Adobe InDesign · Photoshop

Scope

Print Editorial Layout Design

01 — Content Design & Layout

Managing print layouts for
a luxury editorial voice

Zoo Magazine is a premium fashion, design, and art publication — a print medium where every centimetre of space carries intentional visual weight. As the designer responsible for content layout, I worked directly in Adobe InDesign to manage the structure, hierarchy, and composition of each page spread.

The work demanded a deep understanding of Zoo's editorial identity — knowing when to let a full-bleed photograph breathe, when to introduce typographic contrast, and how to maintain visual rhythm across an entire issue without the layout ever competing with the content it carries.

Page layout & composition — Typesetting, image placement, and grid management across all editorial sections.

Print production — Preparing files to print-ready specifications, maintaining colour accuracy and bleed settings throughout.

Content management — Coordinating editorial text and photography into a cohesive, publishable layout for each section.

02 — Magazine Covers & Editorial Spreads

Designing the face of
Issues 80 & 81

The magazine cover is the most high-stakes single design in any print publication — it must command attention on a newsstand, communicate the issue's editorial tone at a glance, and feel unmistakably Zoo. For Issues 80 and 81, I designed the covers and the inner editorial spreads, ensuring visual continuity from the first page to the last.

Each spread was considered both as a standalone composition and as part of the broader rhythm of the issue — balancing bold fashion photography with Zoo's signature typographic restraint.

Editorial approach: Zoo's luxury positioning means the design must always serve the content rather than distract from it — the grid is invisible, the hierarchy is felt rather than seen, and the white space is as deliberate as every element on the page.
Issue 81 — Covers
Editorial Spreads

03 — Archive Section · Issue 81

Honouring the legends of
Zoo's journey

One of the most significant design challenges in Issue 81 was the Archive section — a major and special edition feature portraying all the iconic figures who have been part of Zoo Magazine's story since its founding in Berlin in 2003.

The archive demanded a layout that felt both celebratory and reverent — a visual tribute to decades of fashion, art, and culture captured through Zoo's lens. The section required careful balance between density of content and editorial breathing room, ensuring every name and image received the weight it deserved.

Design challenge: Presenting a large volume of archival content — photographs spanning 20+ years, multiple contributors, and different eras of the magazine — within a unified visual language that felt cohesive rather than crowded.

The scope

"Designing for Zoo Magazine means working at the intersection of fashion photography, typography, and editorial storytelling — where the grid is a foundation, not a constraint, and every layout decision is a reflection of the magazine's 20-year legacy."
